article_title: EU Parliament Votes to End Untargeted Mass Scanning of Private Messages in Historic Chat Control Decision
summary: Multiple specific factual claims cannot be verified from the cited sources. Key statistics, vote counts, amendment details, and quotes are either absent from or contradicted by the linked sources.

findings.message: Vote count '458 to 103, with 63 abstentions' is hyperlinked to the EU Parliament plenary briefing page, but that page is a pre-vote briefing that does not contain vote results.
findings.details: The linked EU Parliament URL (europarl.europa.eu/news/en/agenda/plenary-news/2026-03-09/8/...) describes the upcoming vote and names the rapporteur but does not include vote tallies, amendment details, or outcomes. A source reporting the actual vote result is needed.

findings.message: Amendment 5 by Pirate Party MEP Marketa Gregorova is not mentioned in any cited source.
findings.details: The article attributes Amendment 5 to 'Pirate Party MEP Marketa Gregorova of the Greens/EFA group' and states it was 'adopted by a narrow margin,' but none of the three cited sources reference Amendment 5 or Gregorova.

findings.message: Birgit Sippel quote not found in any cited source.
findings.details: The quote 'a responsibility to address the horrific crime of child sexual abuse while safeguarding everyone's fundamental rights' is attributed to rapporteur Sippel but does not appear in the EU Parliament briefing or other cited sources.

findings.message: Four statistics attributed to the EFF article are not present in that source.
findings.details: The article claims the EFF article reports: (1) 99% of chat reports from Meta, (2) nearly half are false positives, (3) 40% of German investigations targeted minors, (4) 50% drop in reports since 2022. None of these figures appear in the cited EFF article from December 2025.

findings.message: Extension to August 3, 2027 not confirmed by cited sources.
findings.details: The EU Parliament source mentions the current April 3, 2026 expiration but does not reference an August 2027 extension date.

findings.message: Trilogue dates (May 4, June 29) not found in any cited source.
Member state positions (Italy as sole exception, Spain/Ireland vs. Germany/Austria/Netherlands) not supported by cited sources.
Parliament position quote about 'proportional and targeted' scanning not found in cited sources.
findings.details: The quoted text 'need to remain proportional and targeted and should not apply to end-to-end encrypted communications' is not present in the EU Parliament briefing page.

content_preview.title: EU Parliament Votes to End Untargeted Mass Scanning of Private Messages in Historic Chat Control Decision
content_preview.summary: The European Parliament voted 458 to 103 on March 11 to restrict scanning of private communications to judicially authorized, targeted investigations, rejecting years of pressure for blanket surveillance of encrypted messaging platforms.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Parliament voted on March 11 to end untargeted mass scanning of private messages across the bloc, marking a decisive turn in one of Europe's most contentious digital rights debates. By a margin of [458 to 103, with 63 abstentions](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/agenda/plenary-news/2026-03-09/8/child-sexual-abuse-online-vote-on-extending-rules-due-to-expire-in-april), MEPs adopted Amendment 5 requiring that any scanning of private communications be strictly limit...

recommendations: Replace the EU Parliament plenary briefing URL with a source that reports the actual vote results (e.g., the official press release or vote record page)
Add a source for the Amendment 5 details and Marketa Gregorova attribution — consider the European Parliament's procedure page or press release
Provide a verifiable source for the Birgit Sippel quote

The four statistics about Meta reports, false positives, German investigations targeting minors, and chat report decline need sourcing — if they come from a different report (e.g., Europarl research briefing, Patrick Breyer's analysis), cite that source instead of attributing them to the EFF article
Add a source for the August 2027 extension date and the trilogue schedule dates
Add a source for the claim about member state positions (Italy, Spain, Ireland vs. Germany, Austria, Netherlands)

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article with clear Overview, What We Know, What We Don't Know, and Analysis sections. Writing quality is high with neutral, professional tone. Word count (780) is appropriate for the News category.
editor_notes.source_verification: FAILED — Three sources were fetched and read in full. The EU Parliament source is a pre-vote plenary briefing that does not contain vote results, amendment details, or MEP quotes. The EFF article (Dec 2025) discusses Chat Control background but does not contain the specific statistics attributed to it. The TechRadar article confirms the Parliament rejected mass scanning but provides no granular details. Multiple critical claims in the article are unsupported by any cited source.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Cannot be confirmed. The overall narrative (EU Parliament rejecting mass scanning of private messages) is consistent across all three sources and is plausible. However, the specific numbers (vote count 458-103-63, Amendment 5, extension to Aug 2027, trilogue dates, statistical claims about false positives and Meta reports) cannot be verified from the provided sources. These may be accurate but require proper sourcing.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism or editorializing detected. No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: No duplicate or overlapping article found in recent publications. Topic is timely and newsworthy.
concerns: The article makes 8+ specific factual claims that cannot be verified from any of the 3 cited sources, raising the possibility of hallucinated details
The EU Parliament source URL is a pre-vote briefing page, not a vote result page — this is a fundamental source mismatch
recommendations: The article needs additional sources that actually document the vote outcome, amendment details, and statistics cited
If the specific numbers are accurate, they likely come from the official vote record, Patrick Breyer's blog, or other EU digital rights coverage — cite those directly
editor_notes.overall_assessment: The article covers a significant and timely topic with strong writing and neutral tone. However, it fails source verification: multiple critical factual claims (vote count, amendment details, statistical data, MEP quotes, timeline dates) cannot be confirmed from the three cited sources. The submission needs revised sourcing before publication.
